---
title: "Day 9 — Warm-up quiz: complexity from code"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Read a snippet, decide its time complexity. The skill: spot nested loops over the input, recognise iterator chains, and distinguish "work per element" from "work per pair of elements." The previous quiz drilled the cost of individual operations; this one drills the cost of whole snippets.

12 questions, simple to gotcha.

- Express each answer in terms of the input sizes (`n`, `m`, `k`) — not absolute numbers.
- *Worst case* is the costliest possible input. *Average case* is the cost averaged over all (or typical) inputs. Unless we say otherwise, give the worst case.
- A single-pass iterator chain costs as much as one walk over the data, regardless of how many `.map`/`.filter` calls you bolt on — it does *not* multiply the cost.

---

## 1. A simple `for` loop

```rust
fn count_gc(seq: &[u8]) -> usize {
    let mut count = 0;
    for &b in seq {
        if b == b'G' || b == b'C' {
            count += 1;
        }
    }
    count
}
```

What's the complexity in terms of `n = seq.len()`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
How many times does the loop body run? What's the cost per iteration?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n).** The loop runs `n` times, each iteration is constant time (one comparison-or, one optional increment). Total: O(n). This is the canonical single-pass linear scan.
:::

---

## 2. An iterator chain doing the same job

```rust
fn count_gc(seq: &[u8]) -> usize {
    seq.iter()
        .filter(|&&b| b == b'G' || b == b'C')
        .count()
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
A [`.filter`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.filter) does no allocation — it just decides whether to yield each element. [`.count`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html#method.count) walks the (filtered) iterator. How many times does each element get visited?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n).** Each element is visited exactly once: filtered (constant work) and then counted if kept (constant work). Identical complexity to the `for`-loop version — iterator chains don't multiply costs, they compose them into a single pass. *Lesson*: a chain like `.iter().filter(...).map(...).count()` is one pass, not three.
:::

---

## 3. Nested loop comparing every pair

```rust
fn count_matches(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> usize {
    let mut count = 0;
    for x in a {
        for y in b {
            if x == y { count += 1; }
        }
    }
    count
}
```

Complexity in terms of `|a|` and `|b|`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
How many times does the innermost line run?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(|a| × |b|)**, which is **O(n²)** if both slices have length `n`. The inner body runs once per (x, y) pair, of which there are `|a| · |b|`. This is the textbook quadratic pattern — and the one to recognise before you write it on a million-element input. If you only care which elements of `a` also appear in `b`, see Q12 for the O(n) fix.
:::

---

## 4. A `HashMap` lookup inside a loop

```rust
use std::collections::HashMap;

fn count_distinct_keys(keys: &[String], lookup: &HashMap<String, u32>) -> usize {
    let mut hits = 0;
    for k in keys {
        if lookup.contains_key(k) { hits += 1; }
    }
    hits
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n = keys.len()` and `m = lookup.len()`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
The loop runs once per key. What's the per-iteration cost of [`HashMap::contains_key`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashMap.html#method.contains_key)?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n) average**, independent of `m`. The loop runs `n` times. Each `contains_key` is O(1) on *average* (hash, jump to bucket, compare key). Total: O(n) on average. The *worst* case is O(n·m) if every key hashes into the same bucket, but with Rust's default hasher you won't see this in practice.
:::

---

## 5. A linear-scan lookup inside a loop

```rust
fn count_in_unsorted(needles: &[u32], haystack: &[u32]) -> usize {
    let mut hits = 0;
    for n in needles {
        if haystack.contains(n) { hits += 1; }
    }
    hits
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n = needles.len()` and `m = haystack.len()`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
This looks like Q4, but with one critical difference: [`<[T]>::contains`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.contains) on a slice is a linear scan, not a hash lookup.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n × m).** The outer loop runs `n` times; each `haystack.contains(n)` is O(m). Identical loop *shape* to Q4 — but the choice of data structure for `haystack` changes the complexity from O(n) to O(n·m). This is the classic "I should have built a `HashSet`" mistake.
:::

---

## 6. Triple-nested loop

```rust
fn waste_time(n: usize) -> u64 {
    let mut total: u64 = 0;
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..n {
            for k in 0..n {
                total = total.wrapping_add((i * j + k) as u64);
            }
        }
    }
    total
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Three independent loops, each running `n` times. The body is constant work. Multiply.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n³).** The innermost line runs `n × n × n = n³` times, each with constant work. Even at `n = 1000` this is 10⁹ operations — pushing one second of compute. At `n = 10_000` it's 10¹² — about 17 minutes. Triple loops scale badly fast.
:::

---

## 7. Sliding window k-mer iteration

```rust
fn count_kmers(seq: &[u8], k: usize) -> usize {
    seq.windows(k).count()
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n = seq.len()` and `k`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
[`<[T]>::windows(k)`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.windows) yields `n − k + 1` overlapping slices. Each yielded slice is a *fat pointer* (pointer + length), not a copy of `k` bytes — what does that mean for the per-window cost?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n).** `windows(k)` produces `n − k + 1` slices, each in O(1) — no copying, the slice is just a `(ptr, len)` pair into the original buffer. `.count()` walks them all. Total: O(n − k + 1) = O(n). The cost would only become O(n·k) if you *examined* every byte of every window (e.g. comparing or hashing the k bytes per step).
:::

---

## 8. Sort then dedup

```rust
fn unique_count(mut v: Vec<u32>) -> usize {
    v.sort();
    v.dedup();
    v.len()
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n = v.len()`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
What's the cost of [`sort`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.slice.html#method.sort)? Of [`dedup`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html#method.dedup)? Add them and keep the larger term.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n log n)** — dominated by the sort. `sort` is O(n log n); `dedup` is O(n) (it walks the (now sorted) Vec and removes *consecutive* duplicates in place); `len` is O(1). Summing: `O(n log n) + O(n) + O(1) = O(n log n)`. *Gotcha*: `dedup` on its own (without sorting first) only removes consecutive duplicates, so for an unsorted Vec it does not give you the set of distinct values.
:::

---

## 9. Binary search after a sort

```rust
fn count_present(mut v: Vec<u32>, queries: &[u32]) -> usize {
    v.sort();
    let mut hits = 0;
    for target in queries {
        if v.binary_search(target).is_ok() {
            hits += 1;
        }
    }
    hits
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n = v.len()` and `m = queries.len()`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
One sort, then `m` binary searches. Add them.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n log n + m log n).** The sort costs O(n log n). Each binary search costs O(log n), and there are `m` of them, so the search phase is O(m log n). Total: O(n log n + m log n). The sort is a *one-off* cost — amortised over enough queries it disappears, which is why "sort once, query many times" is a good pattern.
:::

---

## 10. Naive recursive Fibonacci

```rust
fn fib(n: u32) -> u64 {
    if n < 2 {
        n as u64
    } else {
        fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2)
    }
}
```

Complexity in terms of `n`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Each call spawns two more calls. The recursion tree has a branching factor of about 2 and a depth of `n`. What does that give you?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**Exponential — O(φⁿ)** where φ ≈ 1.618 is the golden ratio (a fact that falls out of the Fibonacci recurrence). For practical mental arithmetic, call it **O(2ⁿ)** — close enough and easier to remember. The same value is recomputed exponentially many times.

A *memoised* version (cache previously computed values in a HashMap or array) is O(n). An iterative version is also O(n) and uses constant extra memory. This is the canonical motivating example for dynamic programming.
:::

---

## 11. Nested loop with an early return

```rust
fn find_pair(n: usize) -> Option<(usize, usize)> {
    for i in 0..n {
        for j in 0..n {
            if (i * j) % 9999 == 7 {
                return Some((i, j));
            }
        }
    }
    None
}
```

What's the worst-case complexity?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
Best case the function returns on the very first match. Worst case, no match exists. What does the loop do then?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(n²) worst case.** If no pair matches, both loops run to completion: `n × n = n²` iterations. The early `return` helps the *best* and *average* cases but does not change the worst case — and Big-O conventionally reports the worst case unless stated otherwise. Don't be fooled by `return` into thinking a nested loop is cheap.
:::

---

## 12. A `HashSet` membership pattern

```rust
use std::collections::HashSet;

fn shared(a: &[u32], b: &[u32]) -> usize {
    let set: HashSet<&u32> = a.iter().collect();
    b.iter().filter(|x| set.contains(x)).count()
}
```

Complexity in terms of `|a|` and `|b|`?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}
This *looks* like Q3 (pairwise comparison) but isn't. What's the cost of building the [`HashSet`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/collections/struct.HashSet.html)? What's the cost of one membership test?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
**O(|a| + |b|) average**, i.e. **O(n)** if both have length `n`. Building the `HashSet` from `a` is O(|a|) — one insert per element, O(1) average each. Filtering `b` through the set is O(|b|), with each `contains` an O(1)-average lookup. Total O(|a| + |b|). The nested-looking structure is misleading — the inner work is constant, not linear, so we get linear total instead of quadratic.

Compare with Q3 (linear-scan `contains` on a slice): same intent, O(n²) cost. Same intent as Q5: O(n·m) cost. Building the right index drops two orders of magnitude.
:::

---

## Where this leads

Most performance work in real code is recognising what shape your code's loops make. A bad O(n²) inside a hot path eats hours; the same algorithm restructured around a `HashMap` or `HashSet` drops it to O(n). The recipe is almost always:

- Spot the nested loop where the inner operation scans a slice
- Replace the inner slice with a `HashSet` or `HashMap` built once outside the outer loop
- Total cost goes from O(n·m) or O(n²) to O(n + m) or O(n)

Tomorrow's exercises (when added) will give you starter code with deliberately bad complexity to fix — the patterns here are exactly the ones you'll be looking for.
